1:
"And he shewed me a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb." The witnesses note the river of the water of life from the throne. On "he shewed me a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal," the grammatical voices note the river. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal" — the angel shows John a river of the water of life, pure and clear as crystal; the figure of the abundant, life-giving grace and blessedness of the new creation (Ezekiel 47:1; Zechariah 14:8; John 4:14); the river of life flows through the holy city.

On "water of life," the witnesses note the life-giving stream. Barnes and Gill note that the "water of life" signifies the fullness of eternal life, grace, and the satisfaction of the soul; the river is the perpetual supply of life and joy to the redeemed; whether the Spirit, or the grace and blessedness flowing from God.

On "proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb," the latin-fathers Bede and the eastern Cyril note the source: the river proceeds "out of the throne of God and of the Lamb" — its source is God and the Lamb; the life and blessedness of the redeemed flow from the throne, from God and Christ together; Bede and Cyril read the river as the grace of the Spirit, or the stream of eternal life, flowing from God and the Lamb to his people. The verse displays the river of the water of life from the throne.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the river — "a pure river of water of life, clear as crystal, proceeding out of the throne of God and of the Lamb," the life-giving river flowing from God and the Lamb (Ezekiel 47:1; John 4:14, Barnes, Robertson); "water of life" the fullness of eternal life and grace (Barnes, Gill); "out of the throne of God and of the Lamb" the source in God and Christ (Bede, Cyril). The verse displays the river of life. The verse marks the river: of life. The verse displays the purity: clear as crystal. The verse displays the source: the throne. The verse displays the giver: God and the Lamb. The vision displays the stream of eternal life from the throne. The verse displays the river: pure. The verse displays the water: of life. The verse displays the throne: its source. The river of the water of life from the throne displays the blessedness of the new creation: a pure river of the water of life, clear as crystal, flows from the throne of God and of the Lamb — the abundant, eternal life and grace of God streaming to the redeemed. The verse displays the river of life, lined with the tree of life (v.2).
2:
"In the midst of the street of it, and on either side of the river, was there the tree of life, which bare twelve manner of fruits, and yielded her fruit every month: and the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations." The witnesses note the tree of life with its twelve fruits and healing leaves. On "in the midst of the street of it, and on either side of the river, was there the tree of life," the grammatical voices note the tree. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "in the midst of the street of it, and on either side of the river, was there the tree of life" — the tree of life (Genesis 2:9; 3:22-24) grows on both sides of the river, in the city's street; the tree, lost in Eden, is restored in the new creation; whether one tree or a row of trees on each bank, the figure of restored, abundant life.

On "which bare twelve manner of fruits, and yielded her fruit every month," the witnesses note the perpetual fruit. Barnes and Ellicott note that the tree "bare twelve manner of fruits (twelve crops), and yielded her fruit every month" — the tree bears fruit continually, a fresh crop every month; the figure of the perpetual, unfailing supply of life and nourishment to the redeemed; never barren, ever fruitful.

On "the leaves of the tree were for the healing of the nations," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the healing leaves: "the leaves of the tree were for the healing (therapeia) of the nations" — the leaves serve for the healing or health of the nations; whether the figure of the perpetual health and well-being of the redeemed (no more sickness or curse), or of the saving, restoring grace that flows to the nations; the tree's leaves minister life and health; the symbol of the perfect, restored wholeness of the redeemed nations. The verse displays the tree of life with its twelve fruits and healing leaves.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the tree — "the tree of life, which bare twelve manner of fruits, and yielded her fruit every month," the tree of life restored, ever-fruitful (Genesis 2:9; Ezekiel 47:12, Barnes, Robertson); "twelve manner of fruits... every month" the perpetual, unfailing supply of life (Barnes, Ellicott); "the leaves... for the healing of the nations," the leaves ministering health and wholeness (Cyril). The verse displays the tree of life. The verse marks the tree: of life. The verse displays the fruit: twelve, monthly. The verse displays the leaves: for healing. The verse displays the nations: made whole. The vision displays the restored tree of life nourishing the redeemed. The verse displays the tree: fruitful. The verse displays the fruit: perpetual. The verse displays the leaves: healing. The tree of life with its twelve fruits and healing leaves displays the restored Eden of the new creation: the tree of life grows by the river, bearing twelve manner of fruits every month, its leaves for the healing of the nations — the perpetual life, nourishment, and wholeness of the redeemed. The verse displays the tree of life, in the curse-free city (v.3).
3:
"And there shall be no more curse: but the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it; and his servants shall serve him:" The witnesses note the curse removed and God's servants serving him. On "there shall be no more curse," the grammatical voices note the reversal of the fall. Barnes and Robertson note the removal: "there shall be no more curse" — the curse pronounced at the fall (Genesis 3:17) is wholly removed in the new creation; no more curse, no more anything accursed (the word also means "no more accursed thing"); the figure of the perfect blessedness and holiness of the new order, the curse of sin abolished.

On "the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it," the witnesses note God's presence. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it" — God and the Lamb reign in the city; the throne (the seat of God's presence and rule) is in the holy city; God and Christ are immediately present and reigning among the redeemed.

On "his servants shall serve him," the grammatical-historical note the service of the redeemed: "his servants shall serve him" — the redeemed serve God (and the Lamb); their blessed estate is not idleness but glad, perpetual service and worship; the servants of God serve him forever in the curse-free city; perfect, joyful service in his presence. The verse displays the curse removed and God's servants serving him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reversal — "there shall be no more curse," the curse of the fall abolished (Genesis 3:17, Barnes, Robertson); "the throne of God and of the Lamb shall be in it," God and the Lamb reigning in the city (Barnes, Ellicott); "his servants shall serve him," the redeemed serving God in glad, perpetual worship (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the curse-free city. The verse marks the curse: removed. The verse displays the throne: of God and the Lamb. The verse displays the servants: serving. The verse displays the service: perpetual. The vision displays the redeemed serving God where the curse is gone. The verse displays the curse: abolished. The verse displays the throne: present. The verse displays the service: glad. The curse removed and God's servants serving him display the blessedness of the new creation: there is no more curse, the throne of God and the Lamb is in the city, and his servants serve him — the curse of the fall abolished, God reigning, the redeemed serving him forever. The verse displays the serving redeemed, who see God's face (v.4).
4:
"And they shall see his face; and his name shall be in their foreheads." The very brief verse notes the redeemed seeing God's face and bearing his name. The grammatical voices note the beatific vision. Barnes and Robertson note the promise: "they shall see his face; and his name shall be in their foreheads" — the redeemed shall see God's face (the beatific vision, the highest blessedness, Matthew 5:8; 1 John 3:2; Psalm 17:15), and his name shall be on their foreheads (marking them as wholly his); the perfect, immediate communion with God and the seal of his ownership.

On "they shall see his face," the eastern Cyril and the witnesses note the vision of God. Barnes and Cyril note that "they shall see his face" — the redeemed behold God directly, face to face (1 Corinthians 13:12); the beatific vision, denied to mortals (Exodus 33:20), is granted to the glorified; the supreme joy and consummation of the redeemed is to see God.

On "his name shall be in their foreheads," the grammatical-historical note the seal: "his name shall be in their foreheads" — God's name is on their foreheads (3:12; 14:1; in contrast to the beast's mark, 13:16); the mark of his ownership and their belonging wholly to him; the redeemed are sealed as God's own, bearing his name openly. The verse displays the redeemed seeing God's face and bearing his name.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the vision — "they shall see his face; and his name shall be in their foreheads," the redeemed beholding God and bearing his name (Matthew 5:8; 1 John 3:2, Barnes, Robertson); "they shall see his face" the beatific vision, the supreme joy (1 Corinthians 13:12, Cyril); "his name shall be in their foreheads," the seal of God's ownership (3:12; 14:1, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the beatific vision. The verse marks the seeing: his face. The verse displays the vision: of God. The verse displays the name: on their foreheads. The verse displays the seal: God's own. The promise displays the redeemed beholding God and sealed as his. The verse displays the face: seen. The verse displays the name: borne. The verse displays the communion: perfect. The redeemed seeing God's face and bearing his name display the consummate blessedness: they shall see his face — the beatific vision — and his name shall be on their foreheads; the perfect, immediate communion with God and the seal of his ownership. The verse displays the God-beholding redeemed, in the light of his presence (v.5).
5:
"And there shall be no night there; and they need no candle, neither light of the sun; for the Lord God giveth them light: and they shall reign for ever and ever." The witnesses note the eternal day and the everlasting reign of the redeemed. On "there shall be no night there; and they need no candle, neither light of the sun," the grammatical voices note the eternal light. Barnes and Robertson note the feature: "there shall be no night there; and they need no candle, neither light of the sun" — there is no night, and no need of lamp or sun (resuming 21:23-25); the created and artificial lights are superseded; perpetual day, without darkness, in the holy city.

On "for the Lord God giveth them light," the witnesses note the divine light. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the Lord God giveth them light" — God himself is the light of the redeemed (21:23; Isaiah 60:19); his presence and glory illumine them forever; no other light is needed where God shines.

On "they shall reign for ever and ever," the grammatical-historical note the everlasting reign: "they shall reign for ever and ever" — the redeemed reign forever (3:21; 5:10; Daniel 7:18, 27); not a thousand years only, but unto the ages of ages; the saints share Christ's eternal reign; the consummate, endless royalty of the redeemed in the new creation. The verse displays the eternal day and the everlasting reign of the redeemed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the light — "there shall be no night there... for the Lord God giveth them light," the eternal day lit by God himself (21:23; Isaiah 60:19, Barnes, Robertson); "they need no candle, neither light of the sun," the created lights superseded (Barnes, Ellicott); "they shall reign for ever and ever," the everlasting reign of the redeemed (5:10; Daniel 7:18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reigning redeemed. The verse marks the night: none. The verse displays the light: God himself. The verse displays the day: perpetual. The verse displays the reign: forever. The vision displays the redeemed in eternal light and reign. The verse displays the night: gone. The verse displays the light: divine. The verse displays the reign: everlasting. The eternal day and the everlasting reign of the redeemed display the consummate blessedness: there is no night, no need of lamp or sun, for the Lord God gives them light, and they shall reign forever and ever — the perpetual light of God's presence and the endless reign of the redeemed. The verse displays the reigning redeemed, as the angel affirms the words are true (v.6).
6:
"And he said unto me, These sayings are faithful and true: and the Lord God of the holy prophets sent his angel to shew unto his servants the things which must shortly be done." The witnesses note the affirmation that the sayings are faithful and true. On "These sayings are faithful and true," the grammatical voices note the seal of authenticity. Barnes and Robertson note the angel's affirmation (beginning the epilogue): "These sayings are faithful and true" — the angel affirms that the words of the prophecy are faithful (trustworthy) and true (genuine); the whole revelation is sealed as God's reliable word; the assurance of its certainty (as 21:5; 19:9).

On "the Lord God of the holy prophets sent his angel," the witnesses note the divine origin. Barnes and Henry note that "the Lord God of the holy prophets sent his angel to shew unto his servants" — the same God who inspired the prophets of old has sent his angel to reveal these things; the revelation stands in the line of the prophetic word, from the God of the prophets; its authority is divine.

On "the things which must shortly be done," the grammatical-historical note the nearness: the angel was sent to show "the things which must shortly (soon) be done" — echoing the opening (1:1); the events are to come to pass soon/quickly; whether of imminent fulfillment or of the certainty and nearness in God's reckoning; the prophecy concerns things shortly to be accomplished. The verse displays the affirmation that the sayings are faithful and true.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the affirmation — "These sayings are faithful and true," the words sealed as trustworthy and genuine (21:5; 19:9, Barnes, Robertson); "the Lord God of the holy prophets sent his angel," the revelation from the God of the prophets, divinely authoritative (Barnes, Henry); "the things which must shortly be done," the nearness of the fulfillment (1:1, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sealed words. The verse marks the sayings: faithful and true. The verse displays the source: the God of the prophets. The verse displays the angel: sent. The verse displays the things: shortly done. The affirmation displays the revelation sealed as God's true word. The verse displays the words: faithful. The verse displays the prophets: their God. The verse displays the fulfillment: near. The affirmation that the sayings are faithful and true displays the seal of the epilogue: these sayings are faithful and true, and the Lord God of the holy prophets sent his angel to show his servants the things which must shortly be done — the revelation authenticated as God's reliable, near-fulfilled word. The verse displays the sealed words, with Christ's promise to come quickly (v.7).
7:
"Behold, I come quickly: blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book." The very brief verse notes Christ's promise to come quickly and the blessing on the keeper. The grammatical voices note the promise. Barnes and Robertson note Christ's word: "Behold, I come quickly: blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book" — Christ himself declares "I come quickly" (the first of three in this chapter, vv.7, 12, 20), and pronounces blessed the one who keeps (heeds, obeys) the words of the prophecy; the promise of his swift coming and the beatitude on the obedient.

On "Behold, I come quickly," the witnesses note the imminence. Barnes and Ellicott note that "I come quickly" — Christ's coming is near and sudden; "quickly" marks the swiftness and certainty of his coming; the perpetual expectation of the church; whether his coming in judgment, in providence, or at the last day.

On "blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book," the grammatical-historical note the beatitude: "blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book" — the sixth beatitude (echoing 1:3); blessed is the one who keeps (treasures and obeys) the words of the prophecy; the book is to be heeded, not merely read; the obedient are blessed. The verse displays Christ's promise to come quickly and the blessing on the keeper.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "Behold, I come quickly: blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book," Christ's swift coming and the blessing on the obedient (vv.12, 20; 1:3, Barnes, Robertson); "I come quickly" the imminence and certainty of his coming (Barnes, Ellicott); "blessed is he that keepeth the sayings," the beatitude on the obedient keeper (1:3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the coming Christ. The verse marks the coming: quickly. The verse displays the blessing: on the keeper. The verse displays the sayings: of the prophecy. The verse displays the obedience: keeping. The promise displays Christ's swift coming and the blessed keeper. The verse displays the coming: near. The verse displays the keeper: blessed. The verse displays the prophecy: kept. Christ's promise to come quickly and the blessing on the keeper display the close of the prophecy: "Behold, I come quickly; blessed is he that keepeth the sayings of the prophecy of this book" — Christ's swift coming and the beatitude on the one who heeds and obeys the words. The verse displays the coming Christ, occasioning John's worship (v.8).
8:
"And I John saw these things, and heard them. And when I had heard and seen, I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel which shewed me these things." The brief verse notes John falling to worship the angel. The grammatical voices note John's attestation and error. Barnes and Robertson note the witness and the mistaken worship: "I John saw these things, and heard them. And when I had heard and seen, I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel which shewed me these things" — John attests that he saw and heard these things (the personal testimony of the seer), and, overwhelmed, falls to worship the angel (as 19:10); the second instance of John's mistaken worship.

On "I John saw these things, and heard them," the witnesses note the attestation. Barnes and JFB note that "I John saw these things, and heard them" — John personally attests the reality of the visions; the eyewitness testimony of the apostle seals the truth of the revelation.

On "I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel," the grammatical-historical note the repeated error: John "fell down to worship before the feet of the angel" — overcome by the glory of the revelation, John again (as 19:10) falls to worship the angel; the repeated error occasions the angel's renewed rebuke (v.9); the impulse to worship the glorious messenger, which must be redirected to God. The verse displays John falling to worship the angel.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the attestation and error — "I John saw these things, and heard them. And when I had heard and seen, I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel," John's eyewitness testimony and his mistaken worship (19:10, Barnes, Robertson); "I John saw these things, and heard them," the apostle's personal attestation (Barnes, JFB); "I fell down to worship before the feet of the angel," the repeated error of worshiping the messenger (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the worshiping John. The verse marks the witness: John saw and heard. The verse displays the attestation: personal. The verse displays the worship: at the angel's feet. The verse displays the error: repeated. The attestation displays John witnessing and mistakenly worshiping. The verse displays the seer: attesting. The verse displays the worship: misdirected. The verse displays the angel: at whose feet. John falling to worship the angel displays the seer's attestation and renewed error: I John saw and heard these things, and fell down to worship before the angel's feet — the eyewitness testimony of the apostle and his repeated impulse to worship the messenger. The verse displays the worshiping John, rebuked by the angel (v.9).
9:
"Then saith he unto me, See thou do it not: for I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren the prophets, and of them which keep the sayings of this book: worship God." The witnesses note the angel refusing worship and directing it to God. On "See thou do it not: for I am thy fellowservant," the grammatical voices note the refusal. Barnes and Robertson note the angel's words (as 19:10): "See thou do it not: for I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren the prophets, and of them which keep the sayings of this book" — the angel forbids the worship, declaring himself a fellow servant of John, the prophets, and all who keep the words of the book; he is a creature, a servant like them, not to be worshiped.

On "I am thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren the prophets," the witnesses note the angel's humility. Barnes and Ellicott note that the angel calls himself "thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren the prophets, and of them which keep the sayings of this book" — he stands with John, the prophets, and the obedient as a fellow servant of God; angels and saints are alike God's servants; none is to be worshiped but God.

On "worship God," the grammatical-historical note the redirection: "worship God" — the angel directs all worship to God alone; the repeated lesson (19:10) that worship belongs to God, not to angels or any creature; the rebuke of angel-worship and the affirmation of worship due to God only. The verse displays the angel refusing worship and directing it to God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the refusal — "See thou do it not: for I am thy fellowservant... worship God," the angel refusing worship as a fellow servant and directing it to God (19:10, Barnes, Robertson); "thy fellowservant, and of thy brethren the prophets," the angel a creature-servant like John and the prophets (Barnes, Ellicott); "worship God," worship due to God alone (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the redirected worship. The verse marks the refusal: do it not. The verse displays the angel: a fellowservant. The verse displays the brethren: the prophets. The verse displays the command: worship God. The rebuke displays worship reserved for God alone. The verse displays the worship: refused. The verse displays the angel: humble. The verse displays the God: alone worshiped. The angel refusing worship and directing it to God displays the renewed rebuke of angel-worship: the angel forbids John's worship as a fellow servant with him, the prophets, and the obedient, and bids him worship God — worship belonging to God alone. The verse displays the corrected John, told not to seal the prophecy (v.10).
10:
"And he saith unto me, Seal not the sayings of the prophecy of this book: for the time is at hand." The brief verse notes the command not to seal the prophecy, for the time is near. The grammatical voices note the open prophecy. Barnes and Robertson note the directive: "Seal not the sayings of the prophecy of this book: for the time is at hand" — the angel bids John not to seal (keep secret) the words of the prophecy; in contrast to Daniel (who was told to seal his words, Daniel 12:4, 9, for the time was distant), John is to leave the prophecy open, for the time is near.

On "Seal not the sayings of the prophecy of this book," the witnesses note the contrast with Daniel. Barnes and Ellicott note that "seal not" — the prophecy is to remain open, accessible, and proclaimed; unlike Daniel's sealed book, this revelation is for present use; the words are to be made known, not hidden.

On "for the time is at hand," the grammatical-historical note the nearness: "for the time is at hand" — the reason the prophecy is not sealed: the time of its fulfillment is near (1:3); the events are not distant but at hand; the nearness of the fulfillment makes the open proclamation fitting; the prophecy is for the church now. The verse displays the command not to seal the prophecy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the directive — "Seal not the sayings of the prophecy of this book: for the time is at hand," the prophecy left open, unlike Daniel's sealed book, for the time is near (Daniel 12:4, Barnes, Robertson); "seal not" the open, accessible prophecy (Barnes, Ellicott); "for the time is at hand," the nearness of the fulfillment (1:3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the open prophecy. The verse marks the command: seal not. The verse displays the contrast: with Daniel. The verse displays the openness: accessible. The verse displays the time: at hand. The directive displays the prophecy left open for the near time. The verse displays the sealing: forbidden. The verse displays the prophecy: open. The verse displays the time: near. The command not to seal the prophecy displays the openness of the revelation: seal not the sayings of the prophecy of this book, for the time is at hand — the prophecy left open and proclaimed, unlike Daniel's sealed book, because the time of fulfillment is near. The verse displays the open prophecy, before the fixity of states (v.11).
11:
"He that is unjust, let him be unjust still: and he which is filthy, let him be filthy still: and he that is righteous, let him be righteous still: and he that is holy, let him be holy still." The witnesses note the fixity of moral states at the end. On "He that is unjust, let him be unjust still: and he which is filthy, let him be filthy still," the grammatical voices note the declaration. Barnes and Robertson note the solemn word: "He that is unjust, let him be unjust still: and he which is filthy, let him be filthy still: and he that is righteous, let him be righteous still: and he that is holy, let him be holy still" — at the end, each one's moral state is fixed; the unjust and filthy, the righteous and holy, remain as they are; the declaration of the permanence of character at the consummation, when the time of change is past.

On the fixity, interpreters note the meaning. Barnes and Gill note that this "must refer to the scenes beyond" the present life — to the time when probation is over and character is fixed; there is no fixed, unchangeable condition in this world, but at the end each remains in his state; the solemn truth that the moral condition is sealed at the consummation (not a license to sin, but a declaration of the irreversible fixity of states after the judgment).

On "he that is righteous, let him be righteous still," the grammatical-historical note the perseverance of the holy: the righteous and holy remain righteous and holy forever; their state, too, is fixed in blessedness; the permanence of the redeemed in righteousness, as of the lost in their sin; the great divide is made final. The verse displays the fixity of moral states at the end.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fixity — "He that is unjust, let him be unjust still... he that is holy, let him be holy still," each one's moral state fixed at the end (Barnes, Robertson); the declaration referring to the scenes beyond, when character is sealed and probation over (Barnes, Gill); the righteous and holy fixed forever in blessedness, as the lost in sin (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the sealed states. The verse marks the unjust: unjust still. The verse displays the filthy: filthy still. The verse displays the righteous: righteous still. The verse displays the holy: holy still. The declaration displays moral character fixed at the consummation. The verse displays the wicked: fixed. The verse displays the holy: fixed. The verse displays the divide: final. The fixity of moral states at the end displays the solemn permanence of character: he that is unjust and filthy, and he that is righteous and holy, each remains as he is — the moral condition sealed and irreversible at the consummation, when probation is past. The verse displays the fixed states, with Christ's coming and reward (v.12).
12:
"And, behold, I come quickly; and my reward is with me, to give every man according as his work shall be." The very brief verse notes Christ's coming with his reward according to works. The grammatical voices note the second promise. Barnes and Robertson note Christ's word: "behold, I come quickly; and my reward is with me, to give every man according as his work shall be" — Christ again declares "I come quickly" (the second of three, vv.7, 12, 20), bringing his reward with him to render to every man according to his work; the promise of his swift coming and the just recompense of all (Isaiah 40:10; Matthew 16:27).

On "my reward is with me," the witnesses note the recompense. Barnes and Ellicott note that "my reward is with me" — Christ brings the reward (and recompense) at his coming; he renders to each his due; the rewards and judgments are in his hand.

On "to give every man according as his work shall be," the grammatical-historical note the standard: Christ comes "to give every man according as his work shall be" — the principle of judgment by works (20:12-13; Romans 2:6; Matthew 16:27); each is recompensed according to his deeds; the faithful rewarded, the wicked judged; the just retribution at Christ's coming. The verse displays Christ's coming with his reward according to works.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "behold, I come quickly; and my reward is with me, to give every man according as his work shall be," Christ's swift coming with the recompense of all (Isaiah 40:10; Matthew 16:27, Barnes, Robertson); "my reward is with me" the recompense in Christ's hand (Barnes, Ellicott); "to give every man according as his work shall be," the judgment by works (20:12-13; Romans 2:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the recompensing Christ. The verse marks the coming: quickly. The verse displays the reward: with him. The verse displays the standard: according to works. The verse displays the recompense: to every man. The promise displays Christ coming to reward each according to his work. The verse displays the coming: near. The verse displays the reward: brought. The verse displays the works: judged. Christ's coming with his reward according to works displays the second promise of the chapter: "Behold, I come quickly; and my reward is with me, to give every man according as his work shall be" — Christ's swift coming and the just recompense of all according to their deeds. The verse displays the recompensing Christ, the Alpha and Omega (v.13).
13:
"I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last." The very brief verse notes Christ's self-proclamation as Alpha and Omega. The grammatical voices note the divine title. Barnes and Robertson note the self-designation: "I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last" — Christ proclaims himself Alpha and Omega (the first and last letters of the Greek alphabet), the beginning and the end, the first and the last (1:8, 17; 21:6; Isaiah 44:6); the title of eternity and supremacy; Christ is the eternal one, the origin and goal of all.

On "Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end," the witnesses note the eternity. Barnes and Ellicott note that "Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end" — the comprehensive title of God (21:6) is here claimed by Christ; he is the first and the last, eternal, encompassing all; the title affirms Christ's deity and his sovereignty over all history.

On "the first and the last," the grammatical-historical note the divine claim: "the first and the last" — the title applied to Jehovah (Isaiah 44:6; 48:12) is here Christ's; the threefold designation (Alpha and Omega, beginning and end, first and last) emphatically affirms Christ's eternity and supreme deity; he who comes (v.12) is the eternal Lord. The verse displays Christ's self-proclamation as Alpha and Omega.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the title — "I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last," Christ's self-proclamation as the eternal one (1:8, 17; 21:6; Isaiah 44:6, Barnes, Robertson); "Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end" the comprehensive title of God claimed by Christ (21:6, Barnes, Ellicott); "the first and the last," the divine title of Jehovah applied to Christ (Isaiah 44:6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the eternal Christ. The verse marks the title: Alpha and Omega. The verse displays the eternity: beginning and end. The verse displays the supremacy: first and last. The verse displays the deity: divine title. The proclamation displays Christ the eternal Lord of all. The verse displays the Alpha: and Omega. The verse displays the first: and last. The verse displays the Christ: eternal. Christ's self-proclamation as Alpha and Omega displays his eternal deity: "I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last" — Christ the eternal one, the origin and goal of all, claiming the title of God. The verse displays the eternal Christ, with the blessing on the obedient (v.14).
14:
"Blessed are they that do his commandments, that they may have right to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city." The witnesses note the blessing on the obedient — with a textual crux. On "Blessed are they that do his commandments," interpreters note a textual variant. The Reformed Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley follow the Received Text: "Blessed are they that do his commandments" — the seventh and last beatitude of Revelation; blessed are the obedient, who do God's commandments. The grammatical-historical Ellicott and Robertson (with the critical text, the better manuscripts) read instead "Blessed are they that wash their robes" (hoi plynontes tas stolas auton) — blessed are those who wash their robes (in the Lamb's blood, 7:14); the two readings differ by similar-looking Greek words; the critical editors adopt "wash their robes."

On the blessing's substance, the witnesses note its harmony. Barnes and Ellicott note that whether "do his commandments" or "wash their robes," the blessing is held out to the faithful — the obedient who keep God's commandments, or the redeemed who are cleansed in the Lamb's blood; both describe the true people of God who have right to the city.

On "that they may have right to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city," the grammatical-historical note the privilege: the blessed "may have right (authority, access) to the tree of life, and may enter in through the gates into the city" — they have access to the tree of life (v.2; 2:7) and entrance through the gates into the holy city; the reward of the faithful is the tree of life and the city of God. The verse displays the blessing on the obedient.

The witnesses agree the substance: blessed are the faithful — who do God's commandments (TR) or wash their robes (critical text) — for they have right to the tree of life and entrance into the city (Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson). The reading itself is a textual crux: "do his commandments" (Received Text, KJV — Gill, Clarke, Wesley) vs "wash their robes" (the better MSS, critical text — Ellicott, Robertson, Swete). The verse displays the textual crux. The verse marks the blessing: on the faithful. The verse displays the debate: commandments or robes. The verse displays the right: to the tree of life. The verse displays the entrance: into the city. The blessing displays the faithful given the tree of life and the city. The verse displays the obedient: blessed. The verse displays the tree: their right. The verse displays the city: their entrance. The blessing on the obedient, with its textual crux, displays the seventh beatitude: blessed are they that do his commandments (TR) — or wash their robes (critical text) — that they may have right to the tree of life and enter through the gates into the city; the faithful, obedient and cleansed, given the tree of life and the holy city. The verse displays the blessed faithful, set against the excluded (v.15).
15:
"For without are dogs, and sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie." The brief verse notes the excluded sinners outside the city. The grammatical voices note the catalogue. Barnes and Robertson note the contrast with the blessed (v.14): "For without are dogs, and sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie" — outside the city are the excluded — dogs, sorcerers, fornicators, murderers, idolaters, and all who love and practice falsehood; the catalogue of the unredeemed who have no part in the holy city (cf. 21:8).

On "without are dogs," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Gill note that "dogs" (a figure of the impure, shameless, or those outside the covenant, Philippians 3:2; Deuteronomy 23:18) head the list; the term marks the morally unclean and excluded; the dogs are without, shut out of the city.

On "sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie," the grammatical-historical note the excluded: the sorcerers, fornicators, murderers, idolaters, and lovers of falsehood (as 21:8) are outside; the catalogue of those excluded from the city; "whosoever loveth and maketh a lie" emphasizes that all falsehood is shut out; the holy city admits nothing false or unclean. The verse displays the excluded sinners outside the city.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the excluded — "For without are dogs, and sorcerers, and whoremongers, and murderers, and idolaters, and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie," the catalogue of the unredeemed outside the city (21:8, Barnes, Robertson); "dogs" the impure and shameless, excluded (Philippians 3:2, Barnes, Gill); "sorcerers... and whosoever loveth and maketh a lie," all unclean and false shut out (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the excluded. The verse marks the dogs: without. The verse displays the sinners: catalogued. The verse displays the liars: shut out. The verse displays the exclusion: from the city. The catalogue displays the unredeemed outside the holy city. The verse displays the dogs: excluded. The verse displays the sinners: without. The verse displays the lie: shut out. The excluded sinners outside the city display the solemn contrast to the blessed: without are dogs, sorcerers, fornicators, murderers, idolaters, and all who love and make a lie — the unredeemed excluded from the holy city. The verse displays the excluded, before Jesus's own testimony (v.16).
16:
"I Jesus have sent mine angel to testify unto you these things in the churches. I am the root and the offspring of David, and the bright and morning star." The witnesses note Jesus's testimony and his titles. On "I Jesus have sent mine angel to testify unto you these things in the churches," the grammatical voices note the personal attestation. Barnes and Robertson note Christ's own word: "I Jesus have sent mine angel to testify unto you these things in the churches" — Jesus himself attests that he sent his angel to give this testimony to the churches; the revelation is Christ's own, sent through his angel (1:1); the personal seal of the Lord on the whole book.

On "I am the root and the offspring of David," the witnesses note the Davidic title. Barnes and Ellicott note that Christ is "the root and the offspring of David" — both the source (root) and the descendant (offspring) of David (5:5; Isaiah 11:1, 10; Matthew 22:42-45); he is David's Lord and David's son; the Messiah, of David's line yet David's origin; the fulfillment of the Davidic promise.

On "the bright and morning star," the grammatical-historical note the star-title: Christ is "the bright and morning star" (2:28; Numbers 24:17; 2 Peter 1:19) — the herald of the dawn, the bringer of light and the new day; the morning star that ushers in the eternal day; the figure of Christ as the light and hope of his people, the radiant harbinger of glory. The verse displays Jesus's testimony and his titles.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the testimony — "I Jesus have sent mine angel to testify unto you these things in the churches," Christ's personal attestation of the revelation (1:1, Barnes, Robertson); "the root and the offspring of David," David's Lord and son, the Messiah (5:5; Isaiah 11:1, 10, Barnes, Ellicott); "the bright and morning star," Christ the herald of the eternal dawn (2:28; Numbers 24:17, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the testifying Christ. The verse marks the testimony: Jesus's own. The verse displays the angel: sent. The verse displays the title: root and offspring of David. The verse displays the star: bright and morning. The attestation displays Christ sealing the revelation with his titles. The verse displays the Jesus: testifying. The verse displays the David: root and offspring. The verse displays the star: morning. Jesus's testimony and his titles display the Lord's personal seal on the book: I Jesus have sent my angel to testify these things to the churches — I am the root and offspring of David, and the bright and morning star; Christ attesting the revelation and proclaiming himself the Messiah and the herald of the eternal dawn. The verse displays the testifying Christ, with the great invitation (v.17).
17:
"And the Spirit and the bride say, Come. And let him that heareth say, Come. And let him that is athirst come. And whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely." The witnesses note the great invitation of the Spirit and the bride. On "the Spirit and the bride say, Come," the grammatical voices note the call. Barnes and Robertson note the gracious invitation: "the Spirit and the bride say, Come" — the Holy Spirit and the bride (the church) together say "Come"; the invitation to come to Christ (or for Christ to come); the Spirit and the church join in calling sinners to the water of life, and/or in longing for the Lord's coming.

On "let him that heareth say, Come. And let him that is athirst come," the witnesses note the widening call. Barnes and Ellicott note that "let him that heareth say, Come. And let him that is athirst come" — everyone who hears is to join the invitation and to come; the thirsty are bidden to come; the call widens from the Spirit and bride to every hearer and every thirsty soul; the universal gospel invitation.

On "whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely," the grammatical-historical note the free offer: "whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely" — the invitation is open to all who will; the water of life (21:6; Isaiah 55:1; John 7:37) is offered freely, without price; the gracious, universal, free offer of salvation; whoever desires may come and take the water of life. The verse displays the great invitation of the Spirit and the bride.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the invitation — "the Spirit and the bride say, Come... let him that is athirst come. And whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely," the Spirit and the church calling all to come and take the water of life freely (21:6; Isaiah 55:1; John 7:37, Barnes, Robertson); "let him that heareth say, Come" the widening call to every hearer (Barnes, Ellicott); "whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely," the free, universal offer of salvation (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the great invitation. The verse marks the call: the Spirit and bride. The verse displays the widening: every hearer. The verse displays the thirsty: bidden to come. The verse displays the offer: the water of life freely. The invitation displays the free, universal call to salvation. The verse displays the Spirit: calling. The verse displays the bride: inviting. The verse displays the water: free. The great invitation of the Spirit and the bride displays the gospel's open call: the Spirit and the bride say "Come," and let everyone who hears say "Come," and let the thirsty come, and whosoever will, let him take the water of life freely — the gracious, free, universal invitation to salvation. The verse displays the open invitation, with the warning against tampering (vv.18-19).
18:
"For I testify unto every man that heareth the words of the prophecy of this book, If any man shall add unto these things, God shall add unto him the plagues that are written in this book:" The witnesses note the warning against adding to the prophecy. On "I testify unto every man that heareth the words of the prophecy of this book," the grammatical voices note the solemn testimony. Barnes and Robertson note the warning's address: "I testify unto every man that heareth the words of the prophecy of this book" — a solemn testimony (Christ's, or John's) is given to every hearer of the prophecy; the warning that follows binds all who hear the book; the words are not to be tampered with.

On "If any man shall add unto these things," the witnesses note the prohibition of adding. Barnes and Ellicott note that "if any man shall add unto these things" — to add to the words of the prophecy (Deuteronomy 4:2; 12:32) is forbidden; whether of this book specifically, or by extension of the sacred word; the integrity of the prophecy is to be guarded; nothing is to be added.

On "God shall add unto him the plagues that are written in this book," the grammatical-historical note the penalty: "God shall add unto him the plagues that are written in this book" — the one who adds will have the plagues of the book added to him; the solemn penalty for tampering with the prophecy; the judgment threatened on those who corrupt the sacred word; the warning guards the book's integrity. The verse displays the warning against adding to the prophecy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the warning — "I testify unto every man that heareth... If any man shall add unto these things, God shall add unto him the plagues," the solemn warning against adding to the prophecy (Deuteronomy 4:2, Barnes, Robertson); "if any man shall add unto these things" the prohibition of adding (Deuteronomy 12:32, Barnes, Ellicott); "God shall add unto him the plagues," the penalty for tampering (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the testimony: to every hearer. The verse displays the prohibition: add nothing. The verse displays the penalty: the plagues added. The verse displays the integrity: guarded. The warning displays the prophecy guarded against addition. The verse displays the adding: forbidden. The verse displays the plagues: threatened. The verse displays the word: protected. The warning against adding to the prophecy displays the solemn guard on the book: I testify to every hearer — if any man adds to these things, God will add to him the plagues written in this book; the integrity of the prophecy protected against addition by the threat of judgment. The verse displays the guarded word, with the warning against taking away (v.19).
19:
"And if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city, and from the things which are written in this book." The witnesses note the warning against taking from the prophecy. On "if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this prophecy," the grammatical voices note the prohibition of subtracting. Barnes and Robertson note the second warning: "if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this prophecy" — to take away from (subtract, omit, or remove) the words of the prophecy is forbidden, as adding was (v.18); the integrity of the book is guarded against both addition and subtraction (Deuteronomy 4:2).

On "God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city," the witnesses note the penalty. Barnes and Gill note that "God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city, and from the things which are written in this book" — the one who takes away will have his part taken from the book of life, the holy city, and the blessings of the book; the loss of salvation, the city, and the promises; the solemn penalty for corrupting the prophecy by subtraction.

On the warning, the grammatical-historical note the guarded word: the twofold warning (vv.18-19) solemnly guards the integrity of the prophecy; neither addition nor subtraction is permitted; the sacred word is to be preserved entire; the judgment threatened protects the book's purity; whoever tampers with it forfeits its blessings. The verse displays the warning against taking from the prophecy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the warning — "if any man shall take away from the words of the book of this prophecy, God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city," the warning against subtracting from the prophecy, with the loss of salvation and the city (Deuteronomy 4:2, Barnes, Robertson); "take away from the words" the prohibition of subtracting (Barnes, Ellicott); "God shall take away his part out of the book of life, and out of the holy city," the penalty of forfeited blessings (Barnes, Gill). The verse displays the guarded word. The verse marks the prohibition: take nothing away. The verse displays the penalty: part taken from the book of life. The verse displays the loss: the holy city. The verse displays the integrity: guarded. The warning displays the prophecy guarded against subtraction. The verse displays the taking away: forbidden. The verse displays the part: forfeited. The verse displays the word: preserved. The warning against taking from the prophecy displays the second guard on the book: if any man takes away from the words of this prophecy, God will take away his part from the book of life, the holy city, and the blessings written — the integrity of the prophecy protected against subtraction by the threat of forfeited salvation. The verse displays the guarded word, with Christ's final promise (v.20).
20:
"He which testifieth these things saith, Surely I come quickly. Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus." The witnesses note Christ's final promise and the church's answering prayer. On "He which testifieth these things saith, Surely I come quickly," the grammatical voices note the closing promise. Barnes and Robertson note Christ's last word: "He which testifieth these things saith, Surely I come quickly" — Christ, who testifies (v.16), gives his final promise: "Surely (yea) I come quickly" (the third and last of the chapter, vv.7, 12, 20); the closing assurance of his swift, certain coming; the great hope of the church sealed by Christ's own word.

On "Surely I come quickly," the witnesses note the certainty. Barnes and Henry note that "Surely I come quickly" — the emphatic, certain promise of Christ's coming; the last word of the risen Lord in Scripture; the church's hope and longing rest on this assured coming.

On "Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the answering prayer: "Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus" — John (and the church) answers with "Amen" (so be it) and the prayer "Even so, come, Lord Jesus" (Maranatha, 1 Corinthians 16:22); the believing, longing response to Christ's promise; the church echoes his word with the prayer for his coming; the heart of the bride answering the Bridegroom. The verse displays Christ's final promise and the church's answering prayer.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "He which testifieth these things saith, Surely I come quickly," Christ's final, certain promise of his coming (vv.7, 12, Barnes, Robertson); "Surely I come quickly" the emphatic assurance, the church's hope (Barnes, Henry); "Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus," the church's answering prayer for his coming (Maranatha, 1 Corinthians 16:22, Henry). The verse displays the coming Christ and the praying church. The verse marks the promise: I come quickly. The verse displays the certainty: surely. The verse displays the answer: Amen. The verse displays the prayer: come, Lord Jesus. The promise displays Christ's coming answered by the church's longing. The verse displays the coming: sure. The verse displays the Amen: spoken. The verse displays the prayer: Maranatha. Christ's final promise and the church's answering prayer display the close of the book: He who testifies says, "Surely I come quickly"; and the church answers, "Amen. Even so, come, Lord Jesus" — Christ's assured coming and the bride's longing prayer for the Bridegroom. The verse displays the answered promise, with the closing benediction (v.21).
21:
"The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen." The witnesses note the closing benediction of grace. On "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all," the grammatical voices note the apostolic blessing. Barnes and Robertson note the closing words: "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all" — the book (and the whole canon of Scripture) closes with a benediction of grace; the apostolic blessing (as in Paul's letters, e.g. Romans 16:20; 2 Corinthians 13:14) invoking the grace of Christ upon all the readers; the prophecy of judgment and glory ends in grace.

On "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the gospel's heart. Barnes and Ellicott note that "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ" — the unmerited favor and saving kindness of Christ — is invoked; the book that has unfolded the judgments and the glory closes with the grace of Christ, the ground of all the redeemed enjoy; grace is the last word.

On "be with you all. Amen," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the final word: "be with you all. Amen" — the grace of Christ is invoked upon all; the closing "Amen" (so be it) seals the benediction and the whole book; the Bible ends with the prayer of grace and the church's "Amen"; the fitting close of the Scriptures in the grace of the Lord Jesus. The verse displays the closing benediction of grace.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the benediction — "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen," the closing apostolic blessing of grace upon all (Romans 16:20, Barnes, Robertson); "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ" the saving favor of Christ, the gospel's heart (Barnes, Ellicott); "be with you all. Amen," the grace invoked on all, sealed with the church's Amen (Cyril). The verse displays the benediction of grace. The verse marks the grace: of Christ. The verse displays the blessing: be with you all. The verse displays the close: Amen. The verse displays the gospel: grace last. The benediction displays the book closing in the grace of Christ. The verse displays the grace: invoked. The verse displays the all: blessed. The verse displays the Amen: sealed. The closing benediction of grace displays the final word of the book and the Bible: the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen — the prophecy of judgment and glory, and the whole of Scripture, closing in the grace of Christ and the church's Amen. The verse closes the book of Revelation and the canon of Scripture in the grace of the Lord Jesus.
